content.problemStatement:
Chef has come across a mysterious integer and wants to determine whether it is a power of two.

Given an integer inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber, check whether there exists an integer xxx such that:


inputNumber=2xinputNumber = 2^xinputNumber=2x
Return:


true\text{true}true if inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber is a power of two
false\text{false}false otherwise
**Note:**


Negative numbers and zero are **not** powers of two. And do not use any inbuilt language functions.
Function DeclarationDefine a function in a language-independent way as:



**Function Name:**



checkIfPowerOfTwocheckIfPowerOfTwocheckIfPowerOfTwo



**Parameters:**



inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber : a 323232-bit signed integer to be checked



**Return Value:**



A boolean value

true\text{true}true if inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber is a power of two
false\text{false}false otherwise




The implementation **should be efficient** and preferably **should not use loops or recursion**, using an appropriate constant-time check.

Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \leq T \leq 10^{5}1≤T≤105
−231≤inputNumber≤231−1-2^{31} \leq inputNumber \leq 2^{31} - 1−231≤inputNumber≤231−1
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains a single integer inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber.
Output FormatFor each test case:


Print `1` in a single line if inputNumberinputNumberinputNumber is a power of two.
Otherwise, print `0` in a single line.
The output for each test case should be printed on a new line, in the same order as the input.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer inputNumber.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing 1 if inputNumber is a power of two, otherwise 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 1 = 2^0 (power), 2 = 2^1 (power), 3 is not a power of two, 16 = 2^4 (power).
Zero and negative numbers are not considered powers of two.
1073741824 = 2^30, therefore it is a power of two.

solution.approach: A positive integer n is a power of two iff it has exactly one set bit in binary representation. This can be tested in constant time using the expression (n > 0) && ((n & (n - 1)) == 0). The algorithm reads each test case, applies this check, and prints 1 for true and 0 for false. No loops or recursion are needed per test case beyond the outer loop over T.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case is processed in O(1) time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def is_power_of_two(n: int) -> bool:
    return n > 0 and (n & (n - 1)) == 0

data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
T = int(data[0])
out = []
idx = 1
for _ in range(T):
    x = int(data[idx])
    idx += 1
    out.append('1' if is_power_of_two(x) else '0')
print('\n'.join(out))
